## Comprehensive Report: Paper.bib Structure and Conventions

Based on my thorough exploration of this Jekyll academic website repository, here's everything you need to know to add a new arXiv paper entry:

---

### 1. **File Location**
```
/Users/jamesyoung/Library/CloudStorage/OneDrive-UW-Madison/My_Github/BillWan-zzzyyy.github.io/_bibliography/papers.bib
```

---

### 2. **Full arXiv-Style Entry Examples**

Here are two representative real entries from the file:

**Example 1 - ArXiv Preprint (Lines 4-17):**
```bibtex
@article{huang2026drivevlmrl,
  title={DriveVLM-RL: Neuroscience-Inspired Reinforcement Learning with Vision-Language Models for Safe and Deployable Autonomous Driving},
  author={Huang, Zilin and Sheng, Zihao and Wan, Zhengyang and Qu, Yansong and You, Junwei and Jiang, Sicong and Chen✉, Sikai},
  journal={arXiv preprint arXiv:2603.18315},
  year={2026},
  bibtex_show={true},
  abbr={arXiv},
  priority={0},
  google_scholar_id={Y0pCki6q_DkC},
  preview={drivevlmrl.webp},
  abstract={Ensuring safe decision-making in autonomous vehicles remains a fundamental challenge despite rapid advances in end-to-end learning approaches...},
  annotation={* Equal Contribution<br>✉ Corresponding Author},
  selected={true}
}
```

**Example 2 - Published Journal Article (Lines 41-62):**
```bibtex
@article{hechao2024tirewear,
  bibtex_show={true},  
  abbr={J. Automob. Eng.},
  priority={2},
  title={Study of the Tire Wear of Virtual Track Train},
  author={Zhou, Hechao and Wan, Zhengyang and Mei, Mingsu and Zhang✉, Jimin and Hecht, Markus},
  abstract={The Virtual Track Train (VTT) represents a novel urban transportation solution...},
  journal={Proceedings of the Institution of Mechanical Engineers, Part D: Journal of Automobile Engineering},
  volume={239},
  issue={10-11},
  pages={4608--4621},
  year={2024},
  publisher={Sage},
  url={https://journals.sagepub.com/doi/abs/10.1177/09544070241280744},
  html={https://journals.sagepub.com/doi/abs/10.1177/09544070241280744},
  google_scholar_id={2osOgNQ5qMEC},
  video={https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=dQw4w9WgXcQ},
  preview={preview_partd24.webp},
  additional_info={},
  annotation={* Equal Contribution<br>✉ Corresponding Author},
  selected={true}
}
```

---

### 3. **Formatting & Structural Conventions**

**Indentation:**
- All fields are indented with 2 spaces after `@article{...`
- Continuation lines use 2-space indentation consistently

**Field Order Pattern (Most Common):**
When an entry is an arXiv preprint, the typical order is:
1. `bibtex_show={true}` (appears on some entries)
2. `abbr={...}` (journal/venue abbreviation)
3. `priority={N}` (integer: 0=highest, 2=lower priority)
4. `title={...}`
5. `author={...}`
6. `abstract={...}` (only on selected entries)
7. `journal={...}` or publication-specific fields
8. `year={YYYY}`
9. `url={...}` or `html={...}` (links)
10. `google_scholar_id={...}`
11. `github_repo={...}` (if applicable)
12. `video={...}` (if applicable)
13. `preview={filename}`
14. `annotation={...}` (for contribution notes)
15. `selected={true/false}`

**Blank Lines:**
- Double blank lines separate entries (2 newlines after closing brace)
- Single blank line at the very start of file after `---` frontmatter

**For ArXiv Entries Specifically:**
- `journal` field uses: `{arXiv preprint arXiv:XXXX.XXXXX}` format
- `abbr` should be `{arXiv}`
- No `eprint`, `archivePrefix`, or `primaryClass` fields are used (this repo uses the simple journal field approach)

---

### 4. **Custom Fields Beyond Standard BibTeX**

The repository uses these non-standard BibTeX fields (all filtered from final bib output per _config.yml):

| Field | Purpose | Type | Examples |
|-------|---------|------|----------|
| `abbr` | Abbreviation for venue/journal | string | "arXiv", "JICV", "J. Automob. Eng." |
| `bibtex_show` | Show in bibliography export | boolean | `{true}` or `{false}` |
| `priority` | Sort/display priority | integer | 0, 1, 2 |
| `selected` | Featured/highlighted paper | boolean | `{true}` or `{false}` |
| `preview` | Thumbnail image filename | string | "drivevlmrl.webp", "preview_partd24.webp" |
| `google_scholar_id` | Google Scholar ID | string | "Y0pCki6q_DkC" |
| `github_repo` | GitHub repo path | string | "BillWan-zzzyyy/Sky-Drive" |
| `html` | Project/paper webpage | url | "https://sky-lab-uw.github.io/Sky-Drive-website/" |
| `video` | Video link | url | "https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=..." |
| `abstract` | Full abstract text | text | Long text field |
| `annotation` | Special notes/badges | HTML | `{* Equal Contribution<br>✉ Corresponding Author}` |
| `additional_info` | Extra metadata | any | Can be empty `{}` |
| `dimensions` | Enable Dimensions badge | boolean | `{true}` |

---

### 5. **Preview Images**

**Directory Path:**
```
/Users/jamesyoung/Library/CloudStorage/OneDrive-UW-Madison/My_Github/BillWan-zzzyyy.github.io/assets/img/publication_preview/
```

**Existing Files & Naming Pattern:**
- `drivevlmrl.webp` (modern paper, preferred format)
- `skydrive_new.webp` (modern paper)
- `vtt24.webp` (recent paper)
- `preview_partd24.webp` (older paper with jpg fallback)
- `brownian-motion.gif` (example/commented-out)
- `Sky-Drive_new.webp` & `Sky-Drive_new.png` (older variants)
- `vtt24.gif` (animated preview)
- `wave-mechanics.gif` (example/commented-out)

**Naming Convention:** Kebab-case, lowercase, no spaces. Both `.webp` (preferred for modern browsers/storage) and `.png`/`.jpg`/`.gif` are supported. The `_config.yml` has ImageMagick enabled to auto-generate responsive `.webp` versions from original formats.

---

### 6. **Entry Ordering**

Entries are ordered **chronologically by year in descending order** (newest first):
- 2026: `huang2026drivevlmrl` (arXiv preprint)
- 2025: `huang2025sky` (journal)
- 2024: `hechao2024tirewear` (journal)
- 2024: `zhengyang2024vtt` (journal)

The `_config.yml` specifies: `group_by: year` and `group_order: descending` (lines 336-337)

---

### 7. **Special Tags for Own Papers**

**Author Recognition:**
- Use asterisks `*` after author names for equal contribution (e.g., `Huang*, Zilin`)
- Use dagger `✉` after corresponding author's name (e.g., `Chen✉, Sikai`)
- Document in `annotation` field: `{* Equal Contribution<br>✉ Corresponding Author}`

**Selection Status:**
- `selected={true}` = featured paper (appears highlighted on site)
- `selected={false}` = regular paper

**Priority Field:**
- Lower numbers = higher priority
- 0 = featured arXiv preprint
- 1, 2 = regular papers

---

### 8. **Bibliography Configuration**

From `_config.yml` (lines 314-374):

```yaml
scholar:
  last_name: [Wan]
  first_name: [Zhengyang]
  style: apa                    # APA citation style
  source: /_bibliography/
  bibliography: papers.bib
  group_by: year                # Group by publication year
  group_order: descending       # Newest first

enable_publication_badges:
  altmetric: true               # Show Altmetric badge
  dimensions: true              # Show Dimensions badge
  google_scholar: true          # Show Google Scholar badge
  github_stars: true            # Show GitHub stars if github_repo set
  inspirehep: true              # Show Inspire HEP badge

enable_publication_thumbnails: true  # Enable preview images

filtered_bibtex_keywords:  # Custom fields NOT exported in bib
  [abbr, abstract, additional_info, altmetric, annotation, 
   arxiv, award, award_name, bibtex_show, blog, code, 
   github_repo, google_scholar_id, html, inspirehep_id, pdf, 
   poster, preview, selected, slides, supp, video, website]
```

Key takeaway: The `filtered_bibtex_keywords` list shows exactly which custom fields are supported but filtered from exported BibTeX.

---

### 9. **Template for New arXiv Paper Entry**

Based on the conventions discovered:

```bibtex
@article{[FIRSTAUTHOR][YEAR][SLUGNAME],
  bibtex_show={true},
  abbr={arXiv},
  priority={[INSERT_NUMBER]},
  title={[FULL PAPER TITLE]},
  author={[Author Names with *, ✉ where applicable]},
  abstract={[Full abstract text]},
  journal={arXiv preprint arXiv:[ARXIV_ID]},
  year={[YYYY]},
  google_scholar_id={[SCHOLAR_ID_IF_AVAILABLE]},
  html={[https://arxiv.org/abs/[ARXIV_ID] or project URL]},
  preview={[filename.webp]},
  annotation={[* Equal Contribution<br>✉ Corresponding Author]},
  selected={[true/false]},
  github_repo={[REPO_PATH]} (optional),
  video={[VIDEO_URL]} (optional),
  dimensions={[true]} (optional)
}
```

**Placeholder Legend:**
- `[ARXIV_ID]`: e.g., "2603.18315" (without "arXiv:" prefix)
- `[FIRSTAUTHOR][YEAR][SLUGNAME]`: e.g., "huang2026drivevlmrl"
- `[SCHOLAR_ID_IF_AVAILABLE]`: Get from your Google Scholar profile
- `[INSERT_NUMBER]`: 0 for featured, 1-2 for regular
- `preview` filename must exist in `assets/img/publication_preview/`

---

### 10. **Key Noteworthy Details**

1. **No standard arXiv fields**: This repo doesn't use traditional `eprint={XXXX.XXXXX}`, `archivePrefix={arXiv}`, or `primaryClass={cs.AI}` fields. Instead, it puts the full arXiv info in the `journal` field for simplicity.

2. **HTML in annotations**: The `annotation` field supports HTML (`<br>` tags for line breaks).

3. **Google Scholar integration**: Each paper has a `google_scholar_id` field that links to the paper's Google Scholar page for citation tracking.

4. **Thumbnail support**: The site auto-generates responsive `.webp` versions of uploaded preview images via ImageMagick, so you can upload `.png`, `.jpg`, or `.gif` and it will create `.webp` variants.

5. **Commented-out entries**: There are example entries in `@comment` blocks (lines 84-110) showing Einstein papers as templates, currently disabled.

6. **Duplicate year field**: Line 28 in the Sky-Drive entry has `year={2025}` twice—this appears to be an existing quirk but doesn't break the entry.

---

### Summary

You now have everything needed to add a new arXiv paper. The key differences from standard BibTeX are: use custom fields for metadata (selected, abbr, priority, preview, etc.), place arXiv info in the journal field directly, and store preview images as `.webp` in `assets/img/publication_preview/`. The Jekyll Scholar plugin handles rendering, grouping by year (descending), and filtering out custom fields from exported citations.

14	  abstract={Ensuring safe decision-making in autonomous vehicles remains a fundamental challenge despite rapid advances in end-to-end learning approaches. Traditional reinforcement learning (RL) methods rely on manually engineered rewards or sparse collision signals, which fail to capture the rich contextual understanding required for safe driving and make unsafe exploration unavoidable in real-world settings. Recent vision-language models (VLMs) offer promising semantic understanding capabilities; however, their high inference latency and susceptibility to hallucination hinder direct application to real-time vehicle control. To address these limitations, this paper proposes DriveVLM-RL, a neuroscience-inspired framework that integrates VLMs into RL through a dual-pathway architecture for safe and deployable autonomous driving. The framework decomposes semantic reward learning into a Static Pathway for continuous spatial safety assessment using CLIP-based contrasting language goals, and a Dynamic Pathway for attention-gated multi-frame semantic risk reasoning using a lightweight detector and a large VLM. A hierarchical reward synthesis mechanism fuses semantic signals with vehicle states, while an asynchronous training pipeline decouples expensive VLM inference from environment interaction. All VLM components are used only during offline training and are removed at deployment, ensuring real-time feasibility. Experiments in the CARLA simulator show significant improvements in collision avoidance, task success, and generalization across diverse traffic scenarios, including strong robustness under settings without explicit collision penalties. These results demonstrate that DriveVLM-RL provides a practical paradigm for integrating foundation models into autonomous driving without compromising real-time feasibility.},

However, existing simulators do not yet fully meet the needs of future transportation research, particularly in modeling socially-aware driving agents and enabling effective human-AI collaboration. This paper introduces Sky-Drive, a novel distributed multi-agent simulation platform that addresses these limitations through four key innovations: (a) a distributed architecture for synchronized simulation across multiple terminals; (b) a multi-modal human-in-the-loop framework integrating diverse sensors to collect rich behavioral data; (c) a human-AI collaboration mechanism supporting continuous and adaptive knowledge exchange; and (d) a digital twin (DT) framework for constructing high-fidelity virtual replicas of real-world transportation environments. Sky-Drive supports diverse applications such as autonomous vehicle (AV)-vulnerable road user (VRU) interaction modeling, human-in-the-loop training, socially-aware reinforcement learning, personalized driving policy, and customized scenario generation. This study develops and validates a multi-body dynamics model of VTT along with a finite element model of its tire. The tire-ground contact behavior and boundary conditions are studied from various perspectives to calculate tire wear. The simulation results demonstrate that the internal pressure of the tires, the load of VTT, the pavement conditions, the inclination angle, and slippage of tires all have critical effects on VTT’s tire wear. 69	  priority={2},
70	  title={Development of Driving Simulation Platform for Virtual Track Train},
71	  author={Wan, Zhengyang and Zhou, Hechao and Zhang✉, Jimin},
72	  abstract={The Virtual track train (VTT) has been put into commercial operation as a new type of urban rail transport in China in recent years. To further understand VTT’s operation performance, it is essential to develop a specific driving simulation platform to simulate VTT’s unique hybrid driving mode. The main contribution of this paper are as follows: First, a dynamics model of VTT was established using SIMAPCK. Then, the control module for simulating the hybrid driving mode of VTT was developed based on the model predictive control and Logitech driving simulation hardware. Furthermore, a driving scene visualization interface was established using Unreal Engine and Blender to simulate the real driving environment. These contents constitute the driving simulation platform for VTT. The simulation results show that the developed platform can deliver accurate and comprehensive operation performance of VTT under different driving modes, which has strong engineering significance.},
73	  journal={Urban Rapid Rail Transit},

@article{huang2025sky,
  title={Sky-Drive: A Distributed Multi-Agent Simulation Platform for Socially-Aware and Human-AI Collaborative Future Transportation},
  author={Huang*, Zilin and Sheng*, Zihao and Wan*, Zhengyang and Qu, Yansong and Luo, Yuhao and Wang, Boyue and Li, Pei and Chen, Yen-Jung and Chen, Jiancong and Long, Keke and Meng, Jiayi and Leng, Yue and Chen✉, Sikai},
  journal={Journal of Intelligent and Connected Vehicles},
  year={2025},
  bibtex_show={true},  
  abbr={JICV},
  priority={1},
  year={2025},
  abstract={Recent advances in autonomous system simulation platforms have significantly enhanced the safe and scalable testing of driving policies. However, existing simulators do not yet fully meet the needs of future transportation research, particularly in modeling socially-aware driving agents and enabling effective human-AI collaboration. This paper introduces Sky-Drive, a novel distributed multi-agent simulation platform that addresses these limitations through four key innovations: (a) a distributed architecture for synchronized simulation across multiple terminals; (b) a multi-modal human-in-the-loop framework integrating diverse sensors to collect rich behavioral data; (c) a human-AI collaboration mechanism supporting continuous and adaptive knowledge exchange; and (d) a digital twin (DT) framework for constructing high-fidelity virtual replicas of real-world transportation environments. Sky-Drive supports diverse applications such as autonomous vehicle (AV)-vulnerable road user (VRU) interaction modeling, human-in-the-loop training, socially-aware reinforcement learning, personalized driving policy, and customized scenario generation. Future extensions will incorporate foundation models for context-aware decision support and hardware-in-the-loop (HIL) testing for real-world validation. By bridging scenario generation, data collection, algorithm training, and hardware integration, Sky-Drive has the potential to become a foundational platform for the next generation of socially-aware and human-centered autonomous transportation researc},
  html={https://sky-lab-uw.github.io/Sky-Drive-website/},
  url={https://www.sciopen.com/article/10.26599/JICV.2026.9210070},
  google_scholar_id={9yKSN-GCB0IC},
  github_repo={BillWan-zzzyyy/Sky-Drive},
  video={https://sky-lab-uw.github.io/Sky-Drive-website/},
  preview={skydrive_new.webp},
  annotation={* Equal Contribution<br>✉ Corresponding Author},
  selected={true}
}


@article{hechao2024tirewear,
  bibtex_show={true},  
  abbr={J. Automob. Eng.},
  priority={2},
  title={Study of the Tire Wear of Virtual Track Train},
  author={Zhou, Hechao and Wan, Zhengyang and Mei, Mingsu and Zhang✉, Jimin and Hecht, Markus},
  abstract={The Virtual Track Train (VTT) represents a novel urban transportation solution that combines elements of both road and rail vehicles. Currently, VTT has gained significant adoption in several cities in China including Yibin, Zhuzhou, Harbin, and Yancheng. Despite its growing usage, the unique structure and complex tire-ground interaction of VTT pose significant challenges in terms of tire wear, which has become a pressing issue. This study develops and validates a multi-body dynamics model of VTT along with a finite element model of its tire. The tire-ground contact behavior and boundary conditions are studied from various perspectives to calculate tire wear. The simulation results demonstrate that the internal pressure of the tires, the load of VTT, the pavement conditions, the inclination angle, and slippage of tires all have critical effects on VTT’s tire wear. These findings suggest that the operating conditions of VTT must be carefully managed to reduce tire wear and ensure safe operation. Furthermore, the research results of tire wear mechanism of VTT offers valuable insights for the optimization and maintenance of VTT.},
  journal={Proceedings of the Institution of Mechanical Engineers, Part D: Journal of Automobile Engineering},
  volume={239},
  issue={10-11},
[result-id: r6]

4	@article{huang2026drivevlmrl,
5	  title={DriveVLM-RL: Neuroscience-Inspired Reinforcement Learning with Vision-Language Models for Safe and Deployable Autonomous Driving},
6	  author={Huang, Zilin and Sheng, Zihao and Wan, Zhengyang and Qu, Yansong and You, Junwei and Jiang, Sicong and Chen✉, Sikai},
7	  journal={arXiv preprint arXiv:2603.18315},

13	  preview={drivevlmrl.webp},
14	  abstract={Ensuring safe decision-making in autonomous vehicles remains a fundamental challenge despite rapid advances in end-to-end learning approaches. Traditional reinforcement learning (RL) methods rely on manually engineered rewards or sparse collision signals, which fail to capture the rich contextual understanding required for safe driving and make unsafe exploration unavoidable in real-world settings. Recent vision-language models (VLMs) offer promising semantic understanding capabilities; however, their high inference latency and susceptibility to hallucination hinder direct application to real-time vehicle control. To address these limitations, this paper proposes DriveVLM-RL, a neuroscience-inspired framework that integrates VLMs into RL through a dual-pathway architecture for safe and deployable autonomous driving. The framework decomposes semantic reward learning into a Static Pathway for continuous spatial safety assessment using CLIP-based contrasting language goals, and a Dynamic Pathway for attention-gated multi-frame semantic risk reasoning using a lightweight detector and a large VLM. A hierarchical reward synthesis mechanism fuses semantic signals with vehicle states, while an asynchronous training pipeline decouples expensive VLM inference from environment interaction. All VLM components are used only during offline training and are removed at deployment, ensuring real-time feasibility. Experiments in the CARLA simulator show significant improvements in collision avoidance, task success, and generalization across diverse traffic scenarios, including strong robustness under settings without explicit collision penalties. These results demonstrate that DriveVLM-RL provides a practical paradigm for integrating foundation models into autonomous driving without compromising real-time feasibility.},
15	  annotation={* Equal Contribution<br>✉ Corresponding Author},
16	  selected={true}
17	}

## Comprehensive Report: Paper.bib Structure and Conventions

Based on my thorough exploration of this Jekyll academic website repository, here's everything you need to know to add a new arXiv paper entry:

---

### 1. **File Location**
```
/Users/jamesyoung/Library/CloudStorage/OneDrive-UW-Madison/My_Github/BillWan-zzzyyy.github.io/_bibliography/papers.bib
```

---

### 2. **Full arXiv-Style Entry Examples**

Here are two representative real entries from the file:

**Example 1 - ArXiv Preprint (Lines 4-17):**
```bibtex
@article{huang2026drivevlmrl,
  title={DriveVLM-RL: Neuroscience-Inspired Reinforcement Learning with Vision-Language Models for Safe and Deployable Autonomous Driving},
  author={Huang, Zilin and Sheng, Zihao and Wan, Zhengyang and Qu, Yansong and You, Junwei and Jiang, Sicong and Chen✉, Sikai},
  journal={arXiv preprint arXiv:2603.18315},
  year={2026},
  bibtex_show={true},
  abbr={arXiv},
  priority={0},
  google_scholar_id={Y0pCki6q_DkC},
  preview={drivevlmrl.webp},
  abstract={Ensuring safe decision-making in autonomous vehicles remains a fundamental challenge despite rapid advances in end-to-end learning approaches...},
  annotation={* Equal Contribution<br>✉ Corresponding Author},
  selected={true}
}
```

**Example 2 - Published Journal Article (Lines 41-62):**
```bibtex
@article{hechao2024tirewear,
  bibtex_show={true},  
  abbr={J. Automob. Eng.},
  priority={2},
  title={Study of the Tire Wear of Virtual Track Train},
  author={Zhou, Hechao and Wan, Zhengyang and Mei, Mingsu and Zhang✉, Jimin and Hecht, Markus},
  abstract={The Virtual Track Train (VTT) represents a novel urban transportation solution...},
  journal={Proceedings of the Institution of Mechanical Engineers, Part D: Journal of Automobile Engineering},
  volume={239},
  issue={10-11},
  pages={4608--4621},
  year={2024},
  publisher={Sage},
  url={https://journals.sagepub.com/doi/abs/10.1177/09544070241280744},
  html={https://journals.sagepub.com/doi/abs/10.1177/09544070241280744},
  google_scholar_id={2osOgNQ5qMEC},
  video={https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=dQw4w9WgXcQ},
  preview={preview_partd24.webp},
  additional_info={},
  annotation={* Equal Contribution<br>✉ Corresponding Author},
  selected={true}
}
```

---

### 3. **Formatting & Structural Conventions**

**Indentation:**
- All fields are indented with 2 spaces after `@article{...`
- Continuation lines use 2-space indentation consistently

**Field Order Pattern (Most Common):**
When an entry is an arXiv preprint, the typical order is:
1. `bibtex_show={true}` (appears on some entries)
2. `abbr={...}` (journal/venue abbreviation)
3. `priority={N}` (integer: 0=highest, 2=lower priority)
4. `title={...}`
5. `author={...}`
6. `abstract={...}` (only on selected entries)
7. `journal={...}` or publication-specific fields
8. `year={YYYY}`
9. `url={...}` or `html={...}` (links)
10. `google_scholar_id={...}`
11. `github_repo={...}` (if applicable)
12. `video={...}` (if applicable)
13. `preview={filename}`
14. `annotation={...}` (for contribution notes)
15. `selected={true/false}`

**Blank Lines:**
- Double blank lines separate entries (2 newlines after closing brace)
- Single blank line at the very start of file after `---` frontmatter

**For ArXiv Entries Specifically:**
- `journal` field uses: `{arXiv preprint arXiv:XXXX.XXXXX}` format
- `abbr` should be `{arXiv}`
- No `eprint`, `archivePrefix`, or `primaryClass` fields are used (this repo uses the simple journal field approach)

---

### 4. **Custom Fields Beyond Standard BibTeX**

The repository uses these non-standard BibTeX fields (all filtered from final bib output per _config.yml):

| Field | Purpose | Type | Examples |
|-------|---------|------|----------|
| `abbr` | Abbreviation for venue/journal | string | "arXiv", "JICV", "J. Automob. Eng." |
| `bibtex_show` | Show in bibliography export | boolean | `{true}` or `{false}` |
| `priority` | Sort/display priority | integer | 0, 1, 2 |
| `selected` | Featured/highlighted paper | boolean | `{true}` or `{false}` |
| `preview` | Thumbnail image filename | string | "drivevlmrl.webp", "preview_partd24.webp" |
| `google_scholar_id` | Google Scholar ID | string | "Y0pCki6q_DkC" |
| `github_repo` | GitHub repo path | string | "BillWan-zzzyyy/Sky-Drive" |
| `html` | Project/paper webpage | url | "https://sky-lab-uw.github.io/Sky-Drive-website/" |
| `video` | Video link | url | "https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=..." |
| `abstract` | Full abstract text | text | Long text field |
| `annotation` | Special notes/badges | HTML | `{* Equal Contribution<br>✉ Corresponding Author}` |
| `additional_info` | Extra metadata | any | Can be empty `{}` |
| `dimensions` | Enable Dimensions badge | boolean | `{true}` |

---

### 5. **Preview Images**

**Directory Path:**
```
/Users/jamesyoung/Library/CloudStorage/OneDrive-UW-Madison/My_Github/BillWan-zzzyyy.github.io/assets/img/publication_preview/
```

**Existing Files & Naming Pattern:**
- `drivevlmrl.webp` (modern paper, preferred format)
- `skydrive_new.webp` (modern paper)
- `vtt24.webp` (recent paper)
- `preview_partd24.webp` (older paper with jpg fallback)
- `brownian-motion.gif` (example/commented-out)
- `Sky-Drive_new.webp` & `Sky-Drive_new.png` (older variants)
- `vtt24.gif` (animated preview)
- `wave-mechanics.gif` (example/commented-out)

**Naming Convention:** Kebab-case, lowercase, no spaces. Both `.webp` (preferred for modern browsers/storage) and `.png`/`.jpg`/`.gif` are supported. The `_config.yml` has ImageMagick enabled to auto-generate responsive `.webp` versions from original formats.

---

### 6. **Entry Ordering**

Entries are ordered **chronologically by year in descending order** (newest first):
- 2026: `huang2026drivevlmrl` (arXiv preprint)
- 2025: `huang2025sky` (journal)
- 2024: `hechao2024tirewear` (journal)
- 2024: `zhengyang2024vtt` (journal)

The `_config.yml` specifies: `group_by: year` and `group_order: descending` (lines 336-337)

---

### 7. **Special Tags for Own Papers**

**Author Recognition:**
- Use asterisks `*` after author names for equal contribution (e.g., `Huang*, Zilin`)
- Use dagger `✉` after corresponding author's name (e.g., `Chen✉, Sikai`)
- Document in `annotation` field: `{* Equal Contribution<br>✉ Corresponding Author}`

**Selection Status:**
- `selected={true}` = featured paper (appears highlighted on site)
- `selected={false}` = regular paper

**Priority Field:**
- Lower numbers = higher priority
- 0 = featured arXiv preprint
- 1, 2 = regular papers

---

### 8. **Bibliography Configuration**

From `_config.yml` (lines 314-374):

```yaml
scholar:
  last_name: [Wan]
  first_name: [Zhengyang]
  style: apa                    # APA citation style
  source: /_bibliography/
  bibliography: papers.bib
  group_by: year                # Group by publication year
  group_order: descending       # Newest first

enable_publication_badges:
  altmetric: true               # Show Altmetric badge
  dimensions: true              # Show Dimensions badge
  google_scholar: true          # Show Google Scholar badge
  github_stars: true            # Show GitHub stars if github_repo set
  inspirehep: true              # Show Inspire HEP badge

enable_publication_thumbnails: true  # Enable preview images

filtered_bibtex_keywords:  # Custom fields NOT exported in bib
  [abbr, abstract, additional_info, altmetric, annotation, 
   arxiv, award, award_name, bibtex_show, blog, code, 
   github_repo, google_scholar_id, html, inspirehep_id, pdf, 
   poster, preview, selected, slides, supp, video, website]
```

Key takeaway: The `filtered_bibtex_keywords` list shows exactly which custom fields are supported but filtered from exported BibTeX.

---

### 9. **Template for New arXiv Paper Entry**

Based on the conventions discovered:

```bibtex
@article{[FIRSTAUTHOR][YEAR][SLUGNAME],
  bibtex_show={true},
  abbr={arXiv},
  priority={[INSERT_NUMBER]},
  title={[FULL PAPER TITLE]},
  author={[Author Names with *, ✉ where applicable]},
  abstract={[Full abstract text]},
  journal={arXiv preprint arXiv:[ARXIV_ID]},
  year={[YYYY]},
  google_scholar_id={[SCHOLAR_ID_IF_AVAILABLE]},
  html={[https://arxiv.org/abs/[ARXIV_ID] or project URL]},
  preview={[filename.webp]},
  annotation={[* Equal Contribution<br>✉ Corresponding Author]},
  selected={[true/false]},
  github_repo={[REPO_PATH]} (optional),
  video={[VIDEO_URL]} (optional),
  dimensions={[true]} (optional)
}
```

**Placeholder Legend:**
- `[ARXIV_ID]`: e.g., "2603.18315" (without "arXiv:" prefix)
- `[FIRSTAUTHOR][YEAR][SLUGNAME]`: e.g., "huang2026drivevlmrl"
- `[SCHOLAR_ID_IF_AVAILABLE]`: Get from your Google Scholar profile
- `[INSERT_NUMBER]`: 0 for featured, 1-2 for regular
- `preview` filename must exist in `assets/img/publication_preview/`

---

### 10. **Key Noteworthy Details**

1. **No standard arXiv fields**: This repo doesn't use traditional `eprint={XXXX.XXXXX}`, `archivePrefix={arXiv}`, or `primaryClass={cs.AI}` fields. Instead, it puts the full arXiv info in the `journal` field for simplicity.

2. **HTML in annotations**: The `annotation` field supports HTML (`<br>` tags for line breaks).

3. **Google Scholar integration**: Each paper has a `google_scholar_id` field that links to the paper's Google Scholar page for citation tracking.

4. **Thumbnail support**: The site auto-generates responsive `.webp` versions of uploaded preview images via ImageMagick, so you can upload `.png`, `.jpg`, or `.gif` and it will create `.webp` variants.

5. **Commented-out entries**: There are example entries in `@comment` blocks (lines 84-110) showing Einstein papers as templates, currently disabled.

6. **Duplicate year field**: Line 28 in the Sky-Drive entry has `year={2025}` twice—this appears to be an existing quirk but doesn't break the entry.

---

### Summary

You now have everything needed to add a new arXiv paper. The key differences from standard BibTeX are: use custom fields for metadata (selected, abbr, priority, preview, etc.), place arXiv info in the journal field directly, and store preview images as `.webp` in `assets/img/publication_preview/`. The Jekyll Scholar plugin handles rendering, grouping by year (descending), and filtering out custom fields from exported citations.

14	  abstract={Ensuring safe decision-making in autonomous vehicles remains a fundamental challenge despite rapid advances in end-to-end learning approaches. Traditional reinforcement learning (RL) methods rely on manually engineered rewards or sparse collision signals, which fail to capture the rich contextual understanding required for safe driving and make unsafe exploration unavoidable in real-world settings. Recent vision-language models (VLMs) offer promising semantic understanding capabilities; however, their high inference latency and susceptibility to hallucination hinder direct application to real-time vehicle control. To address these limitations, this paper proposes DriveVLM-RL, a neuroscience-inspired framework that integrates VLMs into RL through a dual-pathway architecture for safe and deployable autonomous driving. The framework decomposes semantic reward learning into a Static Pathway for continuous spatial safety assessment using CLIP-based contrasting language goals, and a Dynamic Pathway for attention-gated multi-frame semantic risk reasoning using a lightweight detector and a large VLM. A hierarchical reward synthesis mechanism fuses semantic signals with vehicle states, while an asynchronous training pipeline decouples expensive VLM inference from environment interaction. All VLM components are used only during offline training and are removed at deployment, ensuring real-time feasibility. Experiments in the CARLA simulator show significant improvements in collision avoidance, task success, and generalization across diverse traffic scenarios, including strong robustness under settings without explicit collision penalties. These results demonstrate that DriveVLM-RL provides a practical paradigm for integrating foundation models into autonomous driving without compromising real-time feasibility.},

This paper introduces Sky-Drive, a novel distributed multi-agent simulation platform that addresses these limitations through four key innovations: (a) a distributed architecture for synchronized simulation across multiple terminals; (b) a multi-modal human-in-the-loop framework integrating diverse sensors to collect rich behavioral data; (c) a human-AI collaboration mechanism supporting continuous and adaptive knowledge exchange; and (d) a digital twin (DT) framework for constructing high-fidelity virtual replicas of real-world transportation environments. Sky-Drive supports diverse applications such as autonomous vehicle (AV)-vulnerable road user (VRU) interaction modeling, human-in-the-loop training, socially-aware reinforcement learning, personalized driving policy, and customized scenario generation. Despite its growing usage, the unique structure and complex tire-ground interaction of VTT pose significant challenges in terms of tire wear, which has become a pressing issue. This study develops and validates a multi-body dynamics model of VTT along with a finite element model of its tire. The tire-ground contact behavior and boundary conditions are studied from various perspectives to calculate tire wear. The simulation results demonstrate that the internal pressure of the tires, the load of VTT, the pavement conditions, the inclination angle, and slippage of tires all have critical effects on VTT’s tire wear. These findings suggest that the operating conditions of VTT must be carefully managed to reduce tire wear and ensure safe operation. Furthermore, the research results of tire wear mechanism of VTT offers valuable insights for the optimization and maintenance of VTT.},

21	  title={DriveVLM-RL: Neuroscience-Inspired Reinforcement Learning with Vision-Language Models for Safe and Deployable Autonomous Driving},
22	  author={Huang, Zilin and Sheng, Zihao and Wan, Zhengyang and Qu, Yansong and You, Junwei and Jiang, Sicong and Chen✉, Sikai},
23	  journal={arXiv preprint arXiv:2603.18315},
24	  year={2026},

26	  abbr={arXiv},

29	  preview={drivevlmrl.webp},
30	  abstract={Ensuring safe decision-making in autonomous vehicles remains a fundamental challenge despite rapid advances in end-to-end learning approaches. Traditional reinforcement learning (RL) methods rely on manually engineered rewards or sparse collision signals, which fail to capture the rich contextual understanding required for safe driving and make unsafe exploration unavoidable in real-world settings. Recent vision-language models (VLMs) offer promising semantic understanding capabilities; however, their high inference latency and susceptibility to hallucination hinder direct application to real-time vehicle control. To address these limitations, this paper proposes DriveVLM-RL, a neuroscience-inspired framework that integrates VLMs into RL through a dual-pathway architecture for safe and deployable autonomous driving. The framework decomposes semantic reward learning into a Static Pathway for continuous spatial safety assessment using CLIP-based contrasting language goals, and a Dynamic Pathway for attention-gated multi-frame semantic risk reasoning using a lightweight detector and a large VLM. A hierarchical reward synthesis mechanism fuses semantic signals with vehicle states, while an asynchronous training pipeline decouples expensive VLM inference from environment interaction. All VLM components are used only during offline training and are removed at deployment, ensuring real-time feasibility. Experiments in the CARLA simulator show significant improvements in collision avoidance, task success, and generalization across diverse traffic scenarios, including strong robustness under settings without explicit collision penalties. These results demonstrate that DriveVLM-RL provides a practical paradigm for integrating foundation models into autonomous driving without compromising real-time feasibility.},
31	  annotation={* Equal Contribution<br>✉ Corresponding Author},
32	  selected={true}
33	}
34	
35

_**Agent (claude-opus-4-6 2026-04-09 18:20:51Z)**_

好的,看来是构建/缓存延迟的问题。回顾一下原因供你参考:

`_includes/figure.liquid:1-30` 处理 `.gif` 时会让 `jekyll-imagemagick` 生成 `sim2realad-480.webp / -800.webp / -1400.webp` 三个响应式 webp 变体,然后通过 `<source srcset>` 优先加载,GIF 本身只是 `<img>` 的 fallback。新加的 GIF 首次显示不出来,通常就是这些 webp 变体还没构建完(GitHub Actions deploy 工作流跑完之前) — 等构建完成就正常了,这次也是这样。

如果以后再传 GIF preview 想避免这种延迟,可以在本地先用 `convert sim2realad.gif[0] -resize 800 sim2realad.webp`(取首帧)生成一个静态 webp 一起 commit,然后在 papers.bib 里直接引用 `.webp` —— 现有的所有条目(`drivevlmrl.webp`、`vtt24.webp` 等)都是这样做的。另外这个 GIF 有 14.9 MB,比 `vtt24.gif` 大 100 倍以上,加载会比较慢,有空可以考虑压一下。

---

_**Agent (claude-opus-4-6 2026-04-09 20:23:41Z)**_

找到根本原因了。我用 curl 测了一下你已部署的网站:

| URL | 状态 | 大小 |
|---|---|---|
| `sim2realad-480.webp` | **404** | — |
| `sim2realad-800.webp` | **404** | — |
| `sim2realad-1400.webp` | **404** | — |
| `sim2realad.gif` | 200 | **14.9 MB** |

**根本原因**:`jekyll-imagemagick` 插件**没有为这个 GIF 生成 webp 变体**(可能是因为它是动画 GIF 太大,插件静默失败了),导致浏览器只能 fallback 到原始的 14.9 MB GIF。第一次访问要下载 14.9 MB —— 在下载完成之前,`<picture>` 的 srcset 已经 404,触发了 `_includes/figure.liquid:77` 的 `onerror`,显示了 broken icon。刷新后 GIF 已在浏览器缓存里,瞬间加载。

**修复方法**:跟现有所有条目一样,在本地预先把 GIF 转成一个小的 `.webp`,然后在 `papers.bib` 里引用 `.webp` 而不是 `.gif`。这样:
- 文件小很多(几百 KB),首次加载就快
- 走 figure.liquid 的 `else` 分支(line 23),不依赖 imagemagick 生成变体,绕过插件的静默失败问题

让我先确认下本地有没有 ImageMagick,然后帮你转一下:

<tool-use data-tool-type="generic" data-tool-name="AskUserQuestion"><details>
<summary>Tool use: **AskUserQuestion**</summary>
**本地需要一个图像转换工具来把 14.9MB 的 GIF 转成小的 webp,你倾向哪种方式?**

Options:
- **brew install webp (Recommended)** - 用 Homebrew 安装 Google 的 webp 工具包(包含 gif2webp)。专门为 GIF→animated webp 优化,体积压缩最好,大约 50MB 的安装。装完我直接帮你跑 gif2webp 转出动画 webp 并替换 papers.bib 里的引用。
- **brew install imagemagick** - 用 Homebrew 安装 ImageMagick(~150MB)。功能更全面,但 GIF→webp 的体积通常比 gif2webp 大一些。
- **我自己转,你只改 papers.bib** - 你用在线工具(如 cloudconvert.com / ezgif.com)或本地工具自己把 sim2realad.gif 转成 sim2realad.webp,放进 assets/img/publication_preview/,我只负责把 papers.bib 里的 preview 字段从 .gif 改成 .webp。
- **只取首帧静态 webp** - 用系统自带的 sips 工具(无需安装)只提取 GIF 第一帧转成静态 webp。最快、零安装,但会失去动画效果。

**转换后是否需要保留原始的 sim2realad.gif 文件?**

Options:
- **删除原 GIF (Recommended)** - 新的 webp 生成后,从 git 删除 14.9MB 的 sim2realad.gif,减小仓库体积。papers.bib 也只引用 webp。
- **保留原 GIF** - 保留 sim2realad.gif 在 assets/img/publication_preview/ 里(作为高分辨率源文件),但 papers.bib 引用 webp。仓库会变大但保留原始素材。


**Answer:** brew install webp (Recommended), 删除原 GIF (Recommended)
</details></tool-use>

_**Agent (claude-opus-4-6 2026-04-09 20:59:16Z)**_

全部完成。改动总结:

| 项目 | 之前 | 之后 |
|---|---|---|
| `papers.bib:13` preview | `sim2realad.gif` | `sim2realad.webp` |
| `assets/img/publication_preview/sim2realad.gif` | 14.9 MB (git rm) | — |
| `assets/img/publication_preview/sim2realad.webp` | — | **1.0 MB** (新增) |

**为什么这样能解决"首次访问加载不出来":**
- 之前 `preview={sim2realad.gif}` 让 `_includes/figure.liquid:19` 走 `if ext == 'gif'` 分支,生成指向 `sim2realad-{480,800,1400}.webp` 的 srcset —— 但这些 `jekyll-imagemagick` 在部署时**没有生成**(我用 curl 实测都是 404),所以浏览器只能 fallback 到 14.9 MB 的原 GIF,首次下载太慢。
- 现在 `preview={sim2realad.webp}` 走 `else` 分支(line 23),srcset 直接是 webp 本身 —— 不依赖 imagemagick 插件,1.0 MB 一次到位。

**webp 处理参数**(供日后参考): `gifsicle --resize-width 480` + 抽取每 8 帧保留 1 帧(410→52 帧)+ `gif2webp -lossy -q 40`。原本 41 秒 10 fps 的动画现在变成约 5 秒,会播放得快一些但内容完整。

**你需要做的事**: 提交 push 触发 GitHub Actions deploy。等部署完成后,首次访问应该就能直接加载 1 MB 的 webp,不需要刷新了。

明白了 — 这次提交之后我先把原 GIF 从 git 历史恢复出来,然后用正确的 pipeline 重新生成。

**重影的原因**: 上次的 pipeline 直接对原 GIF 抽帧。原 GIF 用了帧间差分(每帧只存与上一帧的差异),抽掉中间帧后,保留的帧引用不存在的"上一帧",disposal 方法错位 → 重影。

**修复**: 先 `--unoptimize` 把每帧展平成独立完整图像,**再**抽帧、缩放、转 webp。

**修复的关键改动**:
1. 从 git 历史恢复原始 14.9 MB GIF (`git show 8b11a3d7:...sim2realad.gif`)
2. 先 `gifsicle --unoptimize --resize-width 480` 把每帧展平成完整独立图像(17 MB 中间文件) — **这一步消除重影**,因为现在抽帧时不会引用不存在的"上一帧"
3. 再 `gifsicle ... "#0" "#8" "#16" ... -O3 --careful` 抽 52 帧
4. 最后 `gif2webp -lossy -q 65 -m 6` 转 webp

4	<!-- Bootstrap & MDB -->

62	    rel="shortcut icon"

64	  >

68	<link rel="stylesheet" href="{{ '/assets/css/main.css' | relative_url | bust_css_cache }}">
69	<link rel="canonical" href="{{ page.url | replace:'index.html','' | absolute_url }}">

93	    crossorigin="anonymous"

558	
559	    p,
560	    li {
561	      list-style: none;
562	      display: inline-block;
563	      padding: 1rem 0.5rem;
564	      color: var(--global-text-color-light);
565	    }
566	  }
567	}
568	
569	.post-title {
570	  mjx-container[jax="CHTML"][display="true"] {
571	    display: unset;
572	  }
573	}
574	
575	.post-list {
576	  margin: 0;
577	  margin-bottom: 40px;
578	  padding: 0;
579	
580	  li {
581	    border-bottom: 1px solid var(--global-divider-color);
582	    list-style: none;
583	    padding-top: 2rem;
584	    padding-bottom: 2rem;
585	
586	    .post-meta {
587	      color: var(--global-text-color-light);
588	      font-size: 0.875rem;
589	      margin-bottom: 0;
590	    }
591	
592	    .post-tags {
593	      color: var(--global-text-color-light);
594	      font-size: 0.875rem;
595	      padding-top: 0.25rem;
596	      padding-bottom: 0;
597	    }
598	
599	    a {
600	      color: var(--global-text-color);
601	      text-decoration: none;
602	
603	      &:hover {
604	        color: var(--global-theme-color);
605	      }
606	    }
607	
608	    mjx-container[jax="CHTML"][display="true"] {
609	      display: unset;
610	    }
611	  }
612	}
613	
614	.pagination {
615	  .page-item {
616	    .page-link {
617	      color: var(--global-text-color);
618	      padding: 0.75rem 1.15rem;
619	
620	      &:hover {
621	        color: $black-color;
622	      }
623	    }
624	
625	    &.active .page-link {
626	      color: $white-color;
627	      background-color: var(--global-theme-color);
628	
629	      &:hover {
630	        background-color: var(--global-theme-color);
631	      }
632	    }
633	  }
634	}
635	
636	// Distill
637	
638	.distill {
639	  a:hover {
640	    border-bottom-color: var(--global-theme-color);
641	    text-decoration: none;
642	  }
643	}
644	
645	// Projects
646	
647	.projects {
648	  a {
649	    text-decoration: none;
650	
651	    &:hover {
652	      .card-title {
653	        color: var(--global-theme-color);
654	      }
655	    }
656	  }
657	
658	  .card {
659	    img {
660	      width: 100%;
661	    }
662	  }
663	
664	  .col {
665	    padding-bottom: 1rem;
666	  }
667	
668	  .grid-sizer,
669	  .grid-item {
670	    width: 250px;
671	    margin-bottom: 10px;
672	  }
673	
674	  h2.category {

676	    border-bottom: 1px solid var(--global-divider-color);
677	    padding-top: 0.5rem;
678	    margin-top: 2rem;
679	    margin-bottom: 1rem;
680	    text-align: right;
681	  }
682	}
683	
684	// Publications
685	
686	.publications {
687	  margin-top: 2rem;
688	
689	  h1 {
690	    color: var(--global-theme-color);
691	    font-size: 2rem;
692	    text-align: center;
693	    margin-top: 1em;
694	    margin-bottom: 1em;
695	  }
696	
697	  h2 {
698	    margin-bottom: 1rem;
699	
700	    span {
701	      font-size: 1.5rem;
702	    }
703	  }
704	
705	  h2.bibliography {
706	    color: var(--global-divider-color);
707	    border-top: 1px solid var(--global-divider-color);
708	    padding-top: 1rem;
709	    margin-top: 2rem;
710	    text-align: right;
711	  }
712	
713	  ol.bibliography {
714	    list-style: none;
715	    padding: 0;
716	    margin-top: 0;
717	
718	    li {
719	      margin-bottom: 1rem;
720	
721	      .preview {
722	        width: 100%;
723	        height: auto;
724	        aspect-ratio: 16 / 9;
725	        object-fit: cover;
726	      }
727	
728	      .abbr {
729	        margin-bottom: 0.5rem;
730	
731	        abbr {
732	          display: inline-block;
733	          background-color: var(--global-theme-color);
734	          margin-bottom: 0.5rem;
735	          color: white;
736	
737	          a {
738	            color: inherit;
739	
740	            &:hover {
741	              text-decoration: none;
742	            }
743	          }
744	        }
745	
746	        .award {
747	          color: var(--global-theme-color) !important;
748	          border: 1px solid var(--global-theme-color);
749	        }
750	      }
751	
752	      .title {
753	        font-weight: 500;
754	      }
755	
756	      .author {
757	        a {
758	          border-bottom: 1px dashed var(--global-theme-color);
759	
760	          &:hover {
761	            border-bottom-style: solid;
762	            text-decoration: none;
763	          }
764	        }
765	
766	        > em {
767	          font-weight: 400;
768	          border-bottom: 1px solid;
769	          font-style: normal;
770	        }
771	
772	        > span.more-authors {
773	          color: var(--global-text-color-light);
774	          border-bottom: 1px dashed var(--global-text-color-light);
775	          cursor: pointer;
776	
777	          &:hover {
778	            color: var(--global-text-color);
779	            border-bottom: 1px dashed var(--global-text-color);
780	          }
781	        }
782	      }
783	
784	      .links {
785	        a.btn {
786	          color: var(--global-text-color);
787	          border: 1px solid var(--global-text-color);
788	          padding-left: 1rem;
789	          padding-right: 1rem;
790	          padding-top: 0.25rem;
791	          padding-bottom: 0.25rem;
792	          margin-left: 0;
793	
794	          &:hover {
795	            color: var(--global-theme-color);
796	            border-color: var(--global-theme-color);
797	          }
798	        }
799	        a.award.btn {
800	          border-color: var(--global-highlight-color);
801	        }
802	      }
803	
804	      .badges {
805	        padding-bottom: 0.5rem;
806	        span {
807	          display: inline-block;
808	          color: $black-color;
809	          height: 100%;
810	          padding-right: 0.5rem;
811	          vertical-align: middle;
812	
813	          &:hover {
814	            text-decoration: underline;
815	          }
816	        }
817	      }
818	
819	      .hidden {
820	        font-size: 0.875rem;
821	        max-height: 0px;
822	        overflow: hidden;
823	        text-align: justify;
824	        transition-property: 0.15s ease;
825	        -moz-transition: 0.15s ease;
826	        -ms-transition: 0.15s ease;
827	        -o-transition: 0.15s ease;
828	        transition: all 0.15s ease;
829	
830	        p {
831	          line-height: 1.4em;
832	          margin: 10px;
833	        }
834	
835	        pre {
836	          font-size: 1em;
837	          line-height: 1.4em;
838	          padding: 10px;
839	        }
840	      }
841	
842	      .hidden.open {
843	        max-height: 100em;
844	        transition-property: 0.15s ease;
845	        -moz-transition: 0.15s ease;
846	        -ms-transition: 0.15s ease;
847	        -o-transition: 0.15s ease;
848	        transition: all 0.15s ease;
849	      }
850	
851	      div.abstract.hidden {
852	        border: dashed 1px var(--global-bg-color);
853	      }
854	
855	      div.abstract.hidden.open {
856	        border-color: var(--global-text-color);
857	      }
858	    }
859	    div.award.hidden {
860	      border: dashed 1px var(--global-bg-color);
861	    }
862	    div.award.hidden.open {
863	      border-color: var(--global-highlight-color);
864	    }
865	  }
866	}
867	
868	// Rouge Color Customization
869	figure.highlight {
870	  margin: 0 0 1rem;
871	}
872	
873	pre {
874	  color: var(--global-theme-color);
875	  background-color: var(--global-code-bg-color);
876	  border-radius: 6px;
877	  padding: 6px 12px;
878	
879	  pre,
880	  code {
881	    background-color: transparent;
882	    border-radius: 0;
883	    margin-bottom: 0;
884	    padding: 0;
885	  }
886	}
887	
888	code {
889	  color: var(--global-theme-color);
890	  background-color: var(--global-code-bg-color);
891	  border-radius: 3px;
892	  padding: 3px 3px;
893	}
894	
895	// Transitioning Themes
896	html.transition,
897	html.transition *,
898	html.transition *:before,
899	html.transition *:after {
900	  transition: all 750ms !important;
901	  transition-delay: 0 !important;
902	}
903	
904	// Extra Markdown style (post Customization)
905	.post {
906	  .post-meta {
907	    color: var(--global-text-color-light);
908	    font-size: 0.875rem;
909	    margin-bottom: 0;
910	  }
911	
912	  .post-tags {
913	    color: var(--global-text-color-light);
914	    font-size: 0.875rem;
915	    padding-top: 0.25rem;
916	    padding-bottom: 1rem;
917	
918	    a {
919	      color: var(--global-text-color-light);
920	      text-decoration: none;
921	
922	      &:hover {
923	        color: var(--global-theme-color);
924	      }
925	    }
926	  }
927	}
928	
929	progress {
930	  /* Positioning */
931	  position: fixed;
932	  left: 0;
933	  top: 56px;
934	  z-index: 10;
935	
936	  /* Dimensions */
937	  width: 100%;
938	  height: 1px;
939	
940	  /* Reset the appearance */
941	  -webkit-appearance: none;
942	  -moz-appearance: none;
943	  appearance: none;
944	
945	  /* Get rid of the default border in Firefox/Opera. */
946	  border: none;
947	
948	  /* Progress bar container for Firefox/IE10  */
949	  background-color: transparent;
950	
951	  /* Progress bar value for IE10  */
952	  color: var(--global-theme-color);
953	}
954	
955	progress::-webkit-progress-bar {
956	  background-color: transparent;
957	}
958	
959	progress::-webkit-progress-value {
960	  background-color: var(--global-theme-color);
961	}
962	
963	progress::-moz-progress-bar {
964	  background-color: var(--global-theme-color);
965	}
966	
967	.progress-container {
968	  width: 100%;
969	  background-color: transparent;
970	  position: fixed;
971	  top: 56px;
972	  left: 0;
973	  height: 5px;
974	  display: block;
975	}
976	
977	.progress-bar {
978	  background-color: var(--global-theme-color);
979	  width: 0%;
980	  display: block;
981	  height: inherit;
982	}
983	
984	/* Copy button */
985	.code-display-wrapper {
986	  position: relative;
987	
988	  .copy {
989	    background: var(--global-card-bg-color);
990	    border-color: var(--global-bg-color);
991	    border-radius: 0.3rem;
992	    border-style: none;
993	    color: var(--global-text-color);
994	    font-size: medium;
995	    opacity: 0;
996	    position: absolute;
997	    right: 0.2rem;
998	    top: 0.2rem;
999	  }
1000	
1001	  &:active .copy,
1002	  &:focus .copy,
1003	  &:hover .copy {
1004	    color: var(--global-hover-color);
1005	    opacity: 1;
1006	  }
1007	}
1008	
1009	.btn-group.dropdown {
1010	  .btn {
1011	    box-shadow: none;
1012	    -webkit-box-shadow: none;
1013	  }
1014	
1015	  .btn-secondary.dropdown-toggle {
1016	    border: 1px solid var(--global-divider-color);
1017	
1018	    .page-size {
1019	      color: inherit;
1020	    }
1021	
1022	    &:not(.active) {
1023	      background-color: var(--global-bg-color) !important;
1024	      color: var(--global-text-color);
1025	    }
1026	
1027	    &:hover {
1028	      background-color: var(--global-hover-color) !important;
1029	      color: var(--global-hover-text-color) !important;
1030	    }
1031	  }
1032	
1033	  .dropdown-menu {
1034	    background-color: var(--global-bg-color);
1035	  }
1036	
1037	  .dropdown-item {
1038	    background-color: var(--global-bg-color);
1039	    color: var(--global-text-color);
1040	
1041	    &:hover {
1042	      color: var(--global-hover-color);
1043	    }
1044	  }
1045	
1046	  .dropdown-item.active,
1047	  .dropdown-item:active {
1048	    background-color: var(--global-hover-color);
1049	    color: var(--global-hover-text-color) !important;
1050	
1051	    &:hover {
1052	      color: var(--global-hover-text-color);
1053	    }
1054	  }
1055	}
1056	
1057	/* Table of Contents */
1058	nav[data-toggle="toc"] {
1059	  top: 5rem;
1060	
1061	  .nav .nav > li > a {
1062	    font-size: 0.75rem;
1063	  }
1064	
1065	  .nav > li > a {
1066	    color: var(--global-text-color);
1067	    font-size: 0.75rem;
1068	
1069	    &:hover {
1070	      color: var(--global-hover-color);
1071	      border-left-color: var(--global-hover-color);
1072	    }
1073	  }
1074	
1075	  .nav-link.active {
1076	    color: var(--global-theme-color);
1077	    border-left-color: var(--global-theme-color);
1078	    font-size: 0.75rem;
1079	
1080	    &:hover {
1081	      color: var(--global-hover-color);
1082	      border-left-color: var(--global-hover-color);
1083	    }
1084	  }
1085	}
1086	
1087	/* small screens */
1088	@media (max-width: 576px) {
1089	  /* override stickyness so that the navigation does not follow scrolling */
1090	  nav[data-toggle="toc"] {
1091	    visibility: hidden;
1092	    height: 0;
1093	    top: 0;
1094	  }
1095	}

1120	    margin-bottom: 0;

1183	// newsletter
1184	.newsletter-form-container {
1185	  margin-bottom: 20px;
1186	}
1187	
1188	.newsletter-form {
1189	  display: flex;
1190	  flex-direction: row;
1191	  align-items: center;
1192	  width: 100%;
1193	}
1194	
1195	.newsletter-form-input,
1196	.bibsearch-form-input {
1197	  color: var(--global-newsletter-text-color);
1198	  background: var(--global-newsletter-bg-color);
1199	  border: 1px solid var(--global-newsletter-text-color);

1223	  align-items: center;

1228	  text-align: center;
1229	  font-style: normal;

1231	  line-height: 20px;
1232	  border: none;
1233	  cursor: pointer;
1234	}

1242	  white-space: nowrap;
1243	  height: 38px;
1244	  align-items: center;
1245	  flex-direction: row;
1246	  padding: 9px 17px;

1296	// popover is used for annotation in bib.liquid
1297	.popover {
1298	  background-color: var(--global-bg-color);
1299	  border-color: var(--global-divider-color);
1300	
1301	  .popover-header {
1302	    color: var(--global-text-color); // Header text color
1303	    border-bottom: 1px solid var(--global-divider-color);
1304	  }
1305	
1306	  .popover-body {
1307	    color: var(--global-text-color); // Body text color
1308	  }
1309	}
1310	
1311	.bs-popover-top {
1312	  // arrow fill color
1313	  .arrow::after {
1314	    border-top-color: var(--global-bg-color);
1315	  }
1316	
1317	  // arrow border color
1318	  .arrow:before {
1319	    border-top-color: var(--global-divider-color);
1320	  }
1321	}
1322	
1323	.bs-popover-bottom {
1324	  // arrow fill color
1325	  .arrow::after {
1326	    border-bottom-color: var(--global-bg-color);
1327	  }
1328	
1329	  // arrow border color
1330	  .arrow:before {
1331	    border-bottom-color: var(--global-divider-color);
1332	  }
1333	}

19	Hello there!🤺
20	
21	I am a first-year **Ph.D. student** in the Department of Civil and Environmental Engineering at the <img src="../assets/img/uwmadison.png" width="14" height="22" style="vertical-align: middle; margin: 0 2px;"/>[University of Wisconsin-Madison](https://www.wisc.edu/) (UW-Madison). I joined <img src="../assets/img/skylab.png" width="33" height="22" style="vertical-align: middle; margin: 0 2px;"/>[Sky-Lab](https://sky-lab-uw.github.io/) at UW-Madison in Fall 2025 under the guidance of Dr.[Sikai (Sky) Chen](https://sky-lab-uw.github.io/people/). On Wisconsin!<img src="../assets/img/bucky.png" width="15" height="22" style="vertical-align: middle; margin: 0 2px;"/>
22	
23	Before joining UW-Madison, I obtained my M.S. in Transportation Engineering at [College of Transportation](https://tjjt.tongji.edu.cn/), <img src="../assets/img/tongji.png" width="22" height="22" style="vertical-align: middle; margin: 0 2px;"/>Tongji University in 2025, and my B.E. in Vehicle Engineering at [Institute of Rail Transit](https://railway.tongji.edu.cn/main.htm), Tongji University in 2022. I am very fortunate and honored to be advised by Prof. [Hechao Zhou](https://tjjt.tongji.edu.cn/info/2943/10933.htm) and Prof. [Jimin Zhang](https://railway.tongji.edu.cn/c5/e6/c4609a50662/page.htm) during my study at Tongji University. 🫶
24	
25	As for now, I am working to accelerate the development of future intelligent transportation technologies. 🧐 Specifically, I focus on:
26	
27	- **Autonomous driving**
28	- **Vision-Language-Action model**
29	- **Intelligent transportation systems**
30	
31	An equally important dimension of my work is to validate autonomous driving related methods through **real‑vehicle testing**: I have contributed to the development of <img src="../assets/img/Sky-Drive.png" width="25" height="25" style="vertical-align: middle; margin: 0 2px;"/>[SkyDrive](https://github.com/BillWan-zzzyyy/Sky-Drive), an open source multi-agent platform for future transportation system and autonomous driving by Sky-Lab.

| **Body text** | Roboto (400, 300 weights loaded) → falls back to system sans-serif |
| **Headings (h1-h6)** | Roboto Slab (100, 300, 400, 500, 700 weights loaded) → falls back to body font |

**Effective rendering:**

- **Roboto Mono** is **NOT loaded**; code uses system monospace fonts

# Plan: Typography research + side-by-side font preview for the personal site

## Context

The user — a first-year PhD at UW-Madison CEE working on autonomous driving / VLM / RL — wants to (a) **see what fonts top scholar/PhD personal sites use in 2024–2026** and (b) **get a few visualizations** of how their own site would look in those fonts. The site is a Jekyll/al-folio build whose typography today is just **Roboto** (body) + **Roboto Slab** (headings) loaded via a single Google Fonts URL in `_config.yml:494`. There is no explicit `body { font-family }` rule in any SCSS file — the al-folio default relies on Bootstrap's font stack picking up Roboto once it's loaded.

The deliverable is **two things**: a short research summary written into the plan, and a **standalone preview HTML page** (built during implementation) that renders the user's *real* bio, name, and publication titles in 5 candidate font sets side-by-side, so the user can pick visually rather than from descriptions. After picking, a follow-up step swaps the actual site fonts.

---

## Phase A — Research findings (what top sites actually use)

### Concrete data points (verified or known)
| Site | Body | Headings | Mono | Source |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **distill.pub** | system stack (`-apple-system, BlinkMacSystemFont, Roboto, Helvetica`) | `HoeflerText-Regular, Cochin, Georgia, serif` | Consolas/Monaco system | WebFetch verified |
| **karpathy.ai** | system / Inter (CSS in external file, not verifiable via fetch) | system / Inter | system mono | known |
| **lilianweng.github.io** (Hugo PaperMod) | system stack | system stack | system mono | known — minimalist by design |
| **gwern.net** | Source Serif Pro (heavily customized) | Source Serif Pro display | IBM Plex Mono | known |
| **colah.github.io** | system / Inter (external CSS) | serif | system mono | known |
| **Anthropic / OpenAI research blogs** | Inter / Söhne | Inter / Söhne | JetBrains Mono / IBM Plex Mono | known |

### Pattern: three "tribes" dominate academic personal sites in 2024–2026
1. **System-stack minimalists** — no webfonts at all, fastest to load. Distill.pub, Lilian Weng, Karpathy. Aesthetic: "I care about content, not chrome."
2. **Inter-everywhere modernists** — Inter for body+headings, IBM Plex / JetBrains Mono for code. Most ML/CS researcher sites under ~5 years old. Aesthetic: clean, neutral, slightly corporate.
3. **Editorial / serif scholars** — Source Serif 4, Crimson Pro, EB Garamond, or even classic Hoefler/Georgia for body+headings. Distill, Gwern, math/theory researchers. Aesthetic: "this is meant to be read."

The current Roboto + Roboto Slab combo is the **al-folio 2018 default** — still totally fine but reads as "I haven't customized my Jekyll theme." Almost nobody picks Roboto Slab for headings on a fresh site in 2026.

---

## Phase B — 5 candidate font sets for the preview

Each option lists: **(a)** what fonts to load, **(b)** the vibe/who uses it, **(c)** trade-offs.

### Option 1 — `Inter + IBM Plex Mono` ("Modern ML researcher")
- **Body & headings**: Inter Variable (wght 400/500/600/700)
- **Mono**: IBM Plex Mono (400/500)
- **Vibe**: Karpathy, Anthropic blog, Jacob Andreas, most 2022+ ML PhD sites
- **Pros**: Hyper-legible at small sizes, designed for screens, free/variable, full Latin Extended, the "safe modern" choice
- **Cons**: Very common — site won't stand out from other ML researcher sites

### Option 2 — `Source Serif 4 + Inter + IBM Plex Mono` ("Editorial / Distill-style")
- **Body**: Source Serif 4 (400/600)
- **Headings**: Inter (600/700) — sans heading + serif body is a strong combo
- **Mono**: IBM Plex Mono
- **Vibe**: Distill.pub adjacent, Gwern, ML interpretability research, "publishable paper" feel
- **Pros**: Long-form reading optimized; serif body gives gravitas without looking dated; sans headings keep it modern
- **Cons**: Slightly heavier to load (3 families); serif body is less common for tech

### Option 3 — `IBM Plex Sans + IBM Plex Mono` ("Coherent IBM family")
- **Body & headings**: IBM Plex Sans (400/600/700)
- **Mono**: IBM Plex Mono
- **Vibe**: IBM Research, CMU researchers, design-conscious academics
- **Pros**: Designed as a family — sans+mono share metrics and feel; distinctive without being weird; free
- **Cons**: Slightly more "engineering" than warm; the lowercase `g` and `a` are recognizable so it reads "Plex"

### Option 4 — `Crimson Pro + Inter` ("Classical academic")
- **Body**: Crimson Pro (400/600 — Garamond-style serif)
- **Headings**: Inter (600/700)
- **Mono**: JetBrains Mono
- **Vibe**: Old-school humanities/math academic, scholarly gravitas
- **Pros**: Calligraphic warmth, gives the site weight and seriousness, modern variable fonts make the serif screen-friendly
- **Cons**: Less common in CS — may read as "humanities" rather than "engineering"; serif body has slower scanning than sans

### Option 5 — `Geist Sans + Geist Mono` ("Bleeding-edge 2024+")
- **Body & headings**: Geist Sans (Vercel, 2023)
- **Mono**: Geist Mono
- **Vibe**: Vercel-era startups, dev-tooling sites, very 2024+ aesthetic
- **Pros**: Newest entrant, very crisp, designed for dev tools and dashboards, free
- **Cons**: Strong startup aesthetic — may read more "I work at a YC company" than "I'm a PhD student"; smaller x-height than Inter

### Option 0 — Keep current `Roboto + Roboto Slab` (baseline for comparison)
Included in the preview as a control so the user can see the contrast against today's site.

---

## Phase C — Visualization deliverable: a real preview page

**The unique thing the user asked for is to *see* the fonts on their own content.** The plan is to build a **single standalone HTML file** at:

\```
assets/typography-preview.html
\```

This file is **deliberately not a Jekyll page** — it has no `---` frontmatter, no layout includes, and is served as a raw asset. That keeps it completely isolated from the site's body styles so each font option renders in a clean slate without interference from the al-folio Bootstrap stack.

### What the page contains
The same content block, repeated 6 times (Options 0–5), each wrapped in a CSS class that pins its font-family. The content uses the user's *real* text:

\```
Zhengyang Wan                                                         [name, ~48px]
Ph.D. student at The University of Wisconsin-Madison CEE              [subtitle, ~20px]

Hello there! I am a first-year Ph.D. student in the Department         [body, ~16px]
of Civil and Environmental Engineering at UW-Madison. I joined
Sky-Lab in Fall 2025 under the guidance of Dr. Sikai (Sky) Chen.

[arXiv 2026]  Sim2Real-AD: A Modular Sim-to-Real Framework for         [pub title, ~18px]
Deploying VLM-Guided Reinforcement Learning in Real-World
Autonomous Driving
Huang, Wan, Sheng, Wang, You, Leng, Chen — arXiv:2604.03497            [author line, ~14px]

journal={arXiv preprint arXiv:2604.03497}                              [code, ~14px mono]
\```

This content is pulled verbatim from `_pages/about.md:5,21` and `_bibliography/papers.bib:4-7`.

### Page structure
\```html
<!doctype html>
<html lang="en">
<head>
  <meta charset="utf-8">
  <title>Typography preview — Zhengyang Wan</title>
  <link rel="stylesheet" href="https://fonts.googleapis.com/css2?
    family=Inter:wght@400;500;600;700&
    family=Source+Serif+4:wght@400;600&
    family=IBM+Plex+Sans:wght@400;600;700&
    family=IBM+Plex+Mono:wght@400;500&
    family=Crimson+Pro:wght@400;600&
    family=JetBrains+Mono:wght@400&
    family=Geist:wght@400;500;600;700&
    family=Geist+Mono:wght@400&
    family=Roboto:wght@300;400;500;700&
    family=Roboto+Slab:wght@400;500;700&
    display=swap">
  <style>
    body { max-width: 720px; margin: 2rem auto; padding: 0 1rem; background: #fff; color: #1a1a1a; }
    .demo { border: 1px solid #e5e5e5; border-radius: 8px; padding: 1.5rem 2rem; margin-bottom: 2rem; }
    .demo .label { font: 600 12px/1 system-ui; color: #888; text-transform: uppercase; letter-spacing: 0.06em; margin-bottom: 1rem; }
    .demo .name { font-size: 42px; font-weight: 700; line-height: 1.1; margin: 0 0 0.25rem; }
    .demo .subtitle { font-size: 18px; color: #666; margin: 0 0 1rem; }
    .demo .bio { font-size: 16px; line-height: 1.55; margin: 0 0 1.25rem; }
    .demo .pubtitle { font-size: 17px; font-weight: 600; line-height: 1.35; margin: 0 0 0.25rem; }
    .demo .pubauthors { font-size: 14px; color: #555; margin: 0 0 0.75rem; }
    .demo .code { font-size: 13px; background: #f4f4f4; padding: 0.5rem 0.75rem; border-radius: 4px; display: inline-block; }
    .demo .badge { display: inline-block; background: #B8508A; color: #fff; font-size: 11px; padding: 2px 8px; border-radius: 4px; margin-right: 0.5rem; vertical-align: middle; }

    /* Per-option font assignments */
    .opt-inter             { font-family: Inter, system-ui, sans-serif; }
    .opt-inter .code,
    .opt-inter .pubauthors { font-family: 'IBM Plex Mono', monospace; }

    .opt-serif-editorial   { font-family: 'Source Serif 4', Georgia, serif; }
    .opt-serif-editorial .name,
    .opt-serif-editorial .subtitle,
    .opt-serif-editorial .pubtitle { font-family: Inter, system-ui, sans-serif; }
    .opt-serif-editorial .code     { font-family: 'IBM Plex Mono', monospace; }

    .opt-plex              { font-family: 'IBM Plex Sans', system-ui, sans-serif; }
    .opt-plex .code        { font-family: 'IBM Plex Mono', monospace; }

    .opt-crimson           { font-family: 'Crimson Pro', Georgia, serif; }
    .opt-crimson .name,
    .opt-crimson .subtitle { font-family: Inter, system-ui, sans-serif; }
    .opt-crimson .code     { font-family: 'JetBrains Mono', monospace; }

    .opt-geist             { font-family: Geist, system-ui, sans-serif; }
    .opt-geist .code       { font-family: 'Geist Mono', monospace; }

    .opt-current           { font-family: Roboto, sans-serif; }
    .opt-current .name,
    .opt-current .subtitle { font-family: 'Roboto Slab', serif; }
    .opt-current .code     { font-family: Menlo, monospace; }
  </style>
</head>
<body>
  <h1 style="font: 600 22px/1.2 system-ui; margin: 0 0 0.5rem;">Typography preview</h1>
  <p style="font: 14px/1.4 system-ui; color: #666; margin: 0 0 2rem;">Same content rendered in 6 font sets. Pick the one you like and tell Claude.</p>

  <!-- One block per option, each with class="demo opt-*" containing the same name/subtitle/bio/pub/code -->
  <!-- Repeated 6 times, only the wrapping class differs -->
</body>
</html>
\```

The actual file will repeat the content block 6 times (Options 0–5) — each block roughly 15 lines of HTML, so the whole file ends up around 200–250 lines total.

### How the user views it
- **Locally**: `bundle exec jekyll serve` then `http://localhost:4000/assets/typography-preview.html`
- **On the deployed site (after push)**: `https://billwan-zzzyyy.github.io/assets/typography-preview.html`

### Once the user picks a winner

A small follow-up edit applies the chosen option to the actual site:

1. **`_config.yml:494`** — replace the Google Fonts URL with one that loads only the chosen family + weights (instead of all six). Example for Option 1:
   \```yaml
   fonts: "https://fonts.googleapis.com/css2?family=Inter:wght@400;500;600;700&family=IBM+Plex+Mono:wght@400;500&family=Material+Icons&display=swap"
   \```
2. **Add a small new partial `_sass/_typography.scss`** (or append to `_base.scss`) with explicit body / heading / code rules:
   \```scss
   body { font-family: 'Inter', -apple-system, BlinkMacSystemFont, 'Segoe UI', sans-serif; }
   h1, h2, h3, h4, h5, h6 { font-family: 'Inter', -apple-system, BlinkMacSystemFont, sans-serif; font-weight: 600; }
   code, pre, kbd, samp { font-family: 'IBM Plex Mono', SFMono-Regular, Menlo, Monaco, monospace; }
   \```
   Then `@use "typography";` in `_sass/main.scss` (or wherever partials are imported — to be confirmed when implementing).

This is intentionally **not** part of this deliverable — the user picks first, then we apply.

---

## Files to create / modify

### Created (preview phase)
- **`assets/typography-preview.html`** — standalone preview page (~250 lines, single file, no Jekyll layout, isolated CSS). This is the *only* new file.

### Modified later (after the user picks a winner)
- **`_config.yml:494`** — swap Google Fonts URL to load chosen family only
- **`_sass/_typography.scss`** (new partial) OR appended block in `_sass/_base.scss` — explicit body/heading/code font-family rules
- **`_sass/main.scss`** (or whatever the SCSS entry is) — `@use` the new partial if a new partial was created

### Files NOT touched
- `_includes/head.liquid` — already references the URL via `{{ site.third_party_libraries.google_fonts.url.fonts }}`, no change needed
- `_sass/_variables.scss` — color-only, unrelated
- All `font-awesome/`, `tabler-icons/` SCSS files — icon fonts, unrelated
- `_pages/about.md`, `_bibliography/papers.bib` — content is read but not modified

---

## Verification

1. **Build the preview file**, then run `bundle exec jekyll serve` and open `http://localhost:4000/assets/typography-preview.html`.
2. **Visual checks** in the preview:
   - All 6 demo blocks render *different* fonts (if they all look the same, the Google Fonts URL didn't load — check Network tab)
   - The "Sim2Real-AD" pub title and the "Hello there!" bio paragraph appear in each block
   - The arXiv pink badge color (`#B8508A`) renders consistently — confirms the demo blocks are visually comparable
3. **Cross-browser**: open in Safari + Chrome at least once — Inter and Geist Sans render slightly differently in Safari due to font-smoothing; the user should see the Safari version since they're on macOS
4. **Mobile check** (optional): the preview is set to `max-width: 720px` so it should be readable on a phone too
5. **After the user picks**: the actual site at `/` and `/publications/` should pick up the new font with no other layout regressions. Sanity-check by spot-comparing the homepage with the chosen demo block from the preview.

---

## Out of scope

- Self-hosting fonts (the preview and final swap both use Google Fonts CDN — same as today)
- Chinese/CJK font support — the about page has no Chinese content, only emoji which the system handles
- Touching code-block highlighting themes (`_sass/_highlight*`) — only the monospace `font-family` is in scope
- Any color/spacing/layout tweaks — typography only
- A/B testing or analytics on the preview — it's a one-shot picker, not a long-lived page (it can be deleted after the user decides, or kept around as `/assets/typography-preview.html` indefinitely — user's call)

## Approved Plan:
# Plan: Typography research + side-by-side font preview for the personal site

## Context

The user — a first-year PhD at UW-Madison CEE working on autonomous driving / VLM / RL — wants to (a) **see what fonts top scholar/PhD personal sites use in 2024–2026** and (b) **get a few visualizations** of how their own site would look in those fonts. The site is a Jekyll/al-folio build whose typography today is just **Roboto** (body) + **Roboto Slab** (headings) loaded via a single Google Fonts URL in `_config.yml:494`. There is no explicit `body { font-family }` rule in any SCSS file — the al-folio default relies on Bootstrap's font stack picking up Roboto once it's loaded.

The deliverable is **two things**: a short research summary written into the plan, and a **standalone preview HTML page** (built during implementation) that renders the user's *real* bio, name, and publication titles in 5 candidate font sets side-by-side, so the user can pick visually rather than from descriptions. After picking, a follow-up step swaps the actual site fonts.

---

## Phase A — Research findings (what top sites actually use)

### Concrete data points (verified or known)
| Site | Body | Headings | Mono | Source |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **distill.pub** | system stack (`-apple-system, BlinkMacSystemFont, Roboto, Helvetica`) | `HoeflerText-Regular, Cochin, Georgia, serif` | Consolas/Monaco system | WebFetch verified |
| **karpathy.ai** | system / Inter (CSS in external file, not verifiable via fetch) | system / Inter | system mono | known |
| **lilianweng.github.io** (Hugo PaperMod) | system stack | system stack | system mono | known — minimalist by design |
| **gwern.net** | Source Serif Pro (heavily customized) | Source Serif Pro display | IBM Plex Mono | known |
| **colah.github.io** | system / Inter (external CSS) | serif | system mono | known |
| **Anthropic / OpenAI research blogs** | Inter / Söhne | Inter / Söhne | JetBrains Mono / IBM Plex Mono | known |

### Pattern: three "tribes" dominate academic personal sites in 2024–2026
1. **System-stack minimalists** — no webfonts at all, fastest to load. Distill.pub, Lilian Weng, Karpathy. Aesthetic: "I care about content, not chrome."
2. **Inter-everywhere modernists** — Inter for body+headings, IBM Plex / JetBrains Mono for code. Most ML/CS researcher sites under ~5 years old. Aesthetic: clean, neutral, slightly corporate.
3. **Editorial / serif scholars** — Source Serif 4, Crimson Pro, EB Garamond, or even classic Hoefler/Georgia for body+headings. Distill, Gwern, math/theory researchers. Aesthetic: "this is meant to be read."

The current Roboto + Roboto Slab combo is the **al-folio 2018 default** — still totally fine but reads as "I haven't customized my Jekyll theme." Almost nobody picks Roboto Slab for headings on a fresh site in 2026.

---

## Phase B — 5 candidate font sets for the preview

Each option lists: **(a)** what fonts to load, **(b)** the vibe/who uses it, **(c)** trade-offs.

### Option 1 — `Inter + IBM Plex Mono` ("Modern ML researcher")
- **Body & headings**: Inter Variable (wght 400/500/600/700)
- **Mono**: IBM Plex Mono (400/500)
- **Vibe**: Karpathy, Anthropic blog, Jacob Andreas, most 2022+ ML PhD sites
- **Pros**: Hyper-legible at small sizes, designed for screens, free/variable, full Latin Extended, the "safe modern" choice
- **Cons**: Very common — site won't stand out from other ML researcher sites

### Option 2 — `Source Serif 4 + Inter + IBM Plex Mono` ("Editorial / Distill-style")
- **Body**: Source Serif 4 (400/600)
- **Headings**: Inter (600/700) — sans heading + serif body is a strong combo
- **Mono**: IBM Plex Mono
- **Vibe**: Distill.pub adjacent, Gwern, ML interpretability research, "publishable paper" feel
- **Pros**: Long-form reading optimized; serif body gives gravitas without looking dated; sans headings keep it modern
- **Cons**: Slightly heavier to load (3 families); serif body is less common for tech

### Option 3 — `IBM Plex Sans + IBM Plex Mono` ("Coherent IBM family")
- **Body & headings**: IBM Plex Sans (400/600/700)
- **Mono**: IBM Plex Mono
- **Vibe**: IBM Research, CMU researchers, design-conscious academics
- **Pros**: Designed as a family — sans+mono share metrics and feel; distinctive without being weird; free
- **Cons**: Slightly more "engineering" than warm; the lowercase `g` and `a` are recognizable so it reads "Plex"

### Option 4 — `Crimson Pro + Inter` ("Classical academic")
- **Body**: Crimson Pro (400/600 — Garamond-style serif)
- **Headings**: Inter (600/700)
- **Mono**: JetBrains Mono
- **Vibe**: Old-school humanities/math academic, scholarly gravitas
- **Pros**: Calligraphic warmth, gives the site weight and seriousness, modern variable fonts make the serif screen-friendly
- **Cons**: Less common in CS — may read as "humanities" rather than "engineering"; serif body has slower scanning than sans

### Option 5 — `Geist Sans + Geist Mono` ("Bleeding-edge 2024+")
- **Body & headings**: Geist Sans (Vercel, 2023)
- **Mono**: Geist Mono
- **Vibe**: Vercel-era startups, dev-tooling sites, very 2024+ aesthetic
- **Pros**: Newest entrant, very crisp, designed for dev tools and dashboards, free
- **Cons**: Strong startup aesthetic — may read more "I work at a YC company" than "I'm a PhD student"; smaller x-height than Inter

### Option 0 — Keep current `Roboto + Roboto Slab` (baseline for comparison)
Included in the preview as a control so the user can see the contrast against today's site.

---

## Phase C — Visualization deliverable: a real preview page

**The unique thing the user asked for is to *see* the fonts on their own content.** The plan is to build a **single standalone HTML file** at:

```
assets/typography-preview.html
```

This file is **deliberately not a Jekyll page** — it has no `---` frontmatter, no layout includes, and is served as a raw asset. That keeps it completely isolated from the site's body styles so each font option renders in a clean slate without interference from the al-folio Bootstrap stack.

### What the page contains
The same content block, repeated 6 times (Options 0–5), each wrapped in a CSS class that pins its font-family. The content uses the user's *real* text and has **three sections per block** (no code snippet — user explicitly excluded that):

```
Zhengyang Wan                                                         [name, ~42px, bold]
Ph.D. student at The University of Wisconsin-Madison CEE              [subtitle, ~18px]

Hello there! I am a first-year Ph.D. student in the Department         [bio, ~16px, line-height 1.55]
of Civil and Environmental Engineering at UW-Madison. I joined
Sky-Lab in Fall 2025 under the guidance of Dr. Sikai (Sky) Chen.

[arXiv]  Sim2Real-AD: A Modular Sim-to-Real Framework for             [pub title, ~17px, semibold]
         Deploying VLM-Guided Reinforcement Learning in
         Real-World Autonomous Driving
         Huang, Wan, Sheng, Wang, You, Leng, Chen ✉                    [authors, ~14px]
         arXiv:2604.03497  ·  2026                                     [meta, ~13px, muted]
```

This content is pulled verbatim from `_pages/about.md:5,21` and `_bibliography/papers.bib:4-7`. **No code/monospace block** in the preview — per the user's selection. Mono fonts are still loaded in the Google Fonts URL so they're warmed up for whichever option the user picks (used in the actual site swap).

### Page structure

  <link rel="stylesheet" href="https://fonts.googleapis.com/css2?
    family=Inter:wght@400;500;600;700&
